answer:
## 1. Trả lời thẳng

**Không. Trên một kết nối DIRECT (P2P), Tailscale, Headscale và NetBird nhanh ngang nhau — chênh lệch tốc độ "trên dây" gần như bằng 0.** Lý do gốc rễ:

- Trên macOS/iOS, Apple **không có kernel WireGuard module**. Cả ba công cụ đều buộc phải chạy **userspace `wireguard-go`** qua interface `utun` ([WireGuard xplatform](https://www.wireguard.com/xplatform/); xác nhận source: `device_darwin.go` của NetBird import `golang.zx2c4.com/wireguard/device`, còn Tailscale dùng `wgengine/userspace.go` + `mem_ios.go` cũng trên `wireguard-go`).
- Cùng cipher (ChaCha20-Poly1305), cùng engine, cùng MTU mặc định 1420, cùng `BatchSize()=1` trên Darwin (không có GSO/GRO offload như Linux — nhưng đó là vấn đề **throughput**, không phải **latency**, và vô nghĩa với traffic terminal).
- Benchmark có kiểm soát (Frikiteam, NetBird tự benchmark) cho thấy chênh lệch latency/throughput chỉ **1–2.5%, nằm trong noise của iperf3** ([Frikiteam](https://docs.frikiteam.es/en/doc/networking/tailscale_netbird_performance/)). **Quan trọng: các benchmark này chạy trên Linux/AWS, không có benchmark macOS nào được công bố** — và trên AWS thì NAT luôn là symmetric nên rất có thể đo trên relay chứ không phải direct.

**Khác biệt thực sự không nằm ở tốc độ gói tin, mà ở XÁC SUẤT giành & giữ được kết nối DIRECT thay vì rơi xuống RELAY.** Đây là "latency cliff": direct ~5–20ms vs relay 50–280ms+ ([Tailscale connection-types](https://tailscale.com/kb/1257/connection-types)). Công cụ nào ép được nhiều kết nối thành direct hơn → "nhanh hơn" về mặt cảm nhận thực tế.

> Lưu ý trung thực: con số ">90% direct" của Tailscale là **internal telemetry tự công bố**, không có audit độc lập ([nat-traversal-improvements-pt-1](https://tailscale.com/blog/nat-traversal-improvements-pt-1)). NetBird **không công bố** con số tương đương. Không có benchmark bên thứ ba nào đo trực tiếp tỉ lệ direct-vs-relay giữa hai bên.

---

## 2. NAT Traversal — ai dùng gì

| Cơ chế | Tailscale / Headscale | NetBird |
|---|---|---|
| **STUN** (khám phá public IP:port) | Có (qua DERP servers) | Có (qua pion/ice + stun.netbird.io) |
| **UDP hole-punching** | Có (DISCO, simultaneous send) | Có (ICE / pion-ice, simultaneous send) |
| **UPnP IGD** | Có (client tự gọi, từ ~2021) | Có — **chỉ từ v0.69.0 (2026-04)** |
| **NAT-PMP** (do Apple tạo ra, RFC 6886) | Có | Có — **chỉ từ v0.69.0** |
| **PCP** (RFC 6887) | Có | Có — **PCP-first từ v0.69.0** (PR #5219) |
| **Birthday-paradox** (cho symmetric/hard NAT) | **Có** (256-port spray) | **Không có** |
| **FreeBSD/pf EIM patch** (hỗ trợ router) | Có (Tailscale tài trợ) | Không |
| **ICE candidate model** | DISCO (riêng, tương đương ICE) | Standard ICE (pion/ice v4) |
| **Relay fallback** | DERP — **TCP/TLS port 443** | NetBird relay — **QUIC (UDP) primary**, WebSocket/TCP fallback |
| **Relay topology** | 20–28 vùng toàn cầu (Tailscale-managed) | Cloud-managed hoặc self-hosted |

**Hai điểm phải nói thẳng (sửa quan niệm cũ):**

1. **Khoảng cách UPnP/NAT-PMP/PCP đã được THU HẸP.** Trước v0.69.0 NetBird hoàn toàn không có port-mapping ([issue #3777](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/3777): "tailscale uses UPnP, PCP and NAT-PMP... NetBird doesn't"). Nhưng PR [#5219](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/pull/5219) (merge 2026-04-15, ship v0.69.0) đã thêm PCP→NAT-PMP→UPnP, có epoch monitoring tự renew khi router reboot. Build gate chỉ là `!js`, **không loại trừ macOS/iOS** ở mức compile. **Nếu bạn đang chạy NetBird cũ hơn v0.69.0 thì lớp này hoàn toàn vắng mặt.**

2. **Tailscale vẫn còn một vũ khí mà NetBird không có: birthday-paradox attack** cho symmetric NAT (đạt 99.9% trong ~20s). Khi cả hai peer sau hard NAT, NetBird rơi thẳng xuống relay; Tailscale còn một đường xác suất để né relay ([how-nat-traversal-works](https://tailscale.com/blog/how-nat-traversal-works)).

---

## 3. UPnP / NAT-PMP / PCP làm gì (và caveat bảo mật)

Cả ba protocol làm cùng một việc: **yêu cầu router tạo một rule forward cố định** `external-port X → internal-ip:port Y`. Tác dụng:

- **Biến hard NAT thành easy NAT** từ góc nhìn của peer: peer luôn biết chính xác địa chỉ để gửi, không cần đua timing hole-punch.
- **Cho một external port ổn định, predictable**, sống sót qua idle periods (NAT-PMP/PCP có lifetime + renew) → giúp **giữ** direct sau sleep/reconnect, không chỉ lúc bắt tay đầu.
- Khắc phục cả lỗi NAT hairpinning trong tình huống same-LAN.

**Caveat bảo mật — đây là lý do UPnP KHÔNG đáng tin làm cơ chế chính:**

- Chính tài liệu Tailscale nói thẳng: *"You can't rely on these protocols being present"* — chúng có thể bị **tắt mặc định**, tắt theo policy, hoặc cả ba bị một toggle duy nhất tắt chung ([how-nat-traversal-works](https://tailscale.com/blog/how-nat-traversal-works)).
- FBI/CISA và nhiều vendor khuyến nghị **tắt UPnP** vì lý do an ninh. Firewall doanh nghiệp và cloud NAT (AWS/Azure) **gần như không bao giờ** hỗ trợ.
- **Apple-specific tốt:** NAT-PMP do chính Apple tạo ra; AirPort/Time Capsule và nhiều router consumer hỗ trợ tốt → trong môi trường nhà/Apple, đây là đường direct khá đáng tin cho Tailscale.
- **Caveat chưa kiểm chứng (load-bearing):** code portmapper được compile vào build iOS/macOS của cả Tailscale (xác nhận qua `autoflags.go`, `iosdeps_test.go` — không có tag `ts_omit_portmapper`) lẫn NetBird. **NHƯNG chưa ai xác nhận bằng packet capture rằng UDP multicast (UPnP) / unicast (NAT-PMP/PCP) discovery có thực sự chạy được bên trong sandbox iOS Network Extension hay không.** Đây là rủi ro runtime, không phải compile-time.

> Tóm lại: khi UPnP bị tắt, **lợi thế Tailscale KHÔNG biến mất** — nó vẫn còn LAN-detect + STUN hole-punch (đủ cho hầu hết home EIM NAT) + birthday-paradox + DERP. Port-mapping là lớp bổ trợ best-effort, không phải cơ chế chính.

---

## 4. Same-LAN vs Cross-network: khi nào đổi VPN mới có ý nghĩa

| Tình huống | Tailscale/Headscale | NetBird (v0.69.0+) | Đổi VPN có đáng? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cùng LAN** (host candidate thắng ngay) | Direct, ~sub-ms | Direct (ICE host candidate ưu tiên cao nhất) | **Không đáng** — cả hai đều direct, latency LAN ngang nhau. (Cảnh báo: NetBird **cũ** có issue same-LAN đi qua srflx/relay khi hairpinning fail — [#1753](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/1753), [#3669](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/3669) — v0.69.0+ với port-mapping có thể đã đỡ.) |
| **Cùng nhà, sau consumer router (UPnP/NAT-PMP on)** | Direct cao | Direct (v0.69.0+); thấp nếu bản cũ | Chỉ đáng nếu đang dùng NetBird cũ |
| **One easy NAT + one CGNAT/cloud** | Direct nhờ port-mapping bên easy | Tùy version | Tailscale nhỉnh hơn |
| **Cả hai sau hard/symmetric NAT** | Relay (nhưng birthday-paradox có cơ hội né) | Relay (rơi thẳng) | Tailscale có cơ hội né relay; nhưng nếu relay thì NetBird QUIC có thể thấp latency hơn DERP TCP |
| **iOS trên cellular (CGNAT)** | Relay (DERP TCP/443) | Relay (QUIC/WebSocket) | Cả hai đều relay; không bên nào "thắng" rõ ràng |
| **Post-sleep reconnect** | Re-upgrade về direct | NetBird **cũ** từng kẹt ở relay phải `down/up` ([#2507](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/2507), [#3936](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/3936)) | Tailscale ổn định hơn về mặt giữ direct |

**Kết luận mục này:** Nếu use-case của bạn **chủ yếu same-LAN**, việc đổi từ NetBird sang Tailscale/Headscale **gần như không cải thiện gì** — cả hai đều direct trên LAN, data plane y hệt. Đổi VPN chỉ thực sự có ý nghĩa khi bạn thường xuyên **cross-network qua NAT khó** (CGNAT, symmetric, cloud) — đó là nơi arsenal traversal rộng hơn của Tailscale (birthday-paradox, port-mapping lâu đời) tạo khác biệt về tỉ lệ direct.

---

## 5. Headscale: khác gì Tailscale-SaaS và NetBird

- **So với Tailscale-SaaS:** Headscale chỉ thay **control plane** (coordination server). Client vẫn là binary Tailscale chính thức → **toàn bộ NAT traversal (magicsock, portmapper UPnP/NAT-PMP/PCP, hole-punch, birthday-paradox, DERP) giống hệt Tailscale** ([headscale.net/derp](https://headscale.net/stable/ref/derp/)). Trên một kết nối direct: **không có khác biệt hiệu năng nào** với Tailscale-SaaS.
  - **Khác biệt thực:** (1) DERP — mặc định Headscale vẫn dùng DERP fleet công cộng của Tailscale; nếu bạn `derp.urls: []` để chỉ tự host thì relay latency phụ thuộc vị trí VPS của bạn, và embedded DERP **"không có tối ưu tốc độ/throughput"** + là single-point-of-failure. (2) Control plane là SPOF — VPS chết thì không tạo được kết nối mới (tunnel đã thiết lập vẫn chạy). (3) Phát hiện node offline có thể chậm tới **~16 phút** (vs Tailscale near-instant) ([routeharden](https://routeharden.com/blog/tailscale-vs-headscale-comparison)). (4) Setup trên Apple cần custom login server (macOS: Debug menu; iOS ≥1.38.1 "custom coordination server").
  - **Đính chính trung thực:** tin đồn regression v0.27.0 ép toàn bộ qua relay (issue [#2846](https://github.com/juanfont/headscale/issues/2846)) — **verdict: refuted.** Issue này được đóng vì *không phải bug của Headscale*; chính reporter xác nhận endpoint vẫn được lưu/propagate đúng (cái "null" là đọc nhầm JSON schema), và lỗi relay tái hiện cả khi trỏ về Tailscale chính thức (nghi do hard-NAT/Windows WireGuard). v0.28.0 không chứa fix cho nó. Để kiểm tra direct: dùng `tailscale debug netmap` (field `Addrs`) + `tailscale status` xem cột direct/relay.

- **So với NetBird:** Headscale = trust model self-hosted (giống NetBird self-hosted) nhưng **được lợi từ arsenal traversal của Tailscale client** (birthday-paradox, port-mapping lâu đời). NetBird là codebase độc lập (pion/ice + relay QUIC). Cả hai đều cho phép self-host control plane đầy đủ.

---

## 6. Khuyến nghị cho use-case (expect P2P, Apple-native, mostly same-LAN / thỉnh thoảng remote)

**Khuyến nghị thẳng: Nếu NetBird hiện tại của bạn đang đạt DIRECT (kiểm tra: `netbird status` thấy P2P chứ không phải relay), thì đừng đổi vì lý do "tốc độ" — sẽ không nhanh hơn.** Data plane y hệt trên Apple.

Cân nhắc theo thứ tự ưu tiên thực tế:

1. **Trước tiên, kiểm chứng bạn có đang direct không**, đừng tin lý thuyết. NetBird: `netbird status` (xem P2P vs relay). Tailscale: `tailscale ping <peer>` / `tailscale status` (cột direct/relay) / `tailscale netcheck` (field PortMapping). Đây là biến quyết định duy nhất cho use-case của bạn.

2. **Nếu chủ yếu same-LAN:** giữ nguyên NetBird là hợp lý. Latency LAN của cả ba ngang nhau, đổi không đem lại gì. **Chỉ đảm bảo NetBird ≥ v0.69.0** để có port-mapping + tránh các bug same-LAN/post-sleep cũ ([#1753](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/1753), [#2507](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/2507)).

3. **Rủi ro iOS — quan trọng nhất cho use-case Apple này:** NetBird có bug iOS data-plane mở (issue [#5789](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/5789), filed 2026-04): handshake/ICE thành công nhưng **0 gói data đi qua utun** (log: `mkdir /var/run/wireguard: operation not permitted`), one-way connectivity, cả WiFi lẫn cellular. Tailscale không có issue tương đương. **Nếu iOS client là path quan trọng, đây là lý do mạnh hơn cả NAT theory để cân nhắc Tailscale/Headscale** — nhưng hãy verify status hiện tại của #5789 trước khi quyết.

4. **Nếu thường xuyên remote qua NAT khó (CGNAT mobile, cloud, symmetric):** Tailscale/Headscale có lợi thế thật nhờ birthday-paradox + port-mapping lâu đời + DERP fleet phân tán → tỉ lệ direct cao hơn và giữ direct ổn định hơn sau reconnect. Nếu cuối cùng vẫn phải relay thì NetBird QUIC relay lại có thể thấp latency hơn DERP TCP — nhưng mục tiêu là né relay hoàn toàn.

5. **Trust model:** muốn self-host + dùng arsenal traversal mạnh nhất → **Headscale + client Tailscale** (lưu ý SPOF control plane + tự lo geography DERP). Muốn self-host nhưng đã quen NetBird → giữ NetBird ≥ v0.69.0.

**Tóm tắt một câu:** Với Apple-native, expect-P2P, **mostly same-LAN** → đổi VPN gần như **không đáng** về tốc độ (data plane bằng nhau, LAN direct cho cả ba). Lý do *chính đáng* duy nhất để chuyển sang Tailscale/Headscale là (a) bug iOS data-plane của NetBird (#5789) nếu iOS là path quan trọng, hoặc (b) bạn thực sự hay rơi vào NAT khó khi remote và cần tỉ lệ direct cao hơn.

Sources chính: [Tailscale how-NAT-works](https://tailscale.com/blog/how-nat-traversal-works), [pt-1](https://tailscale.com/blog/nat-traversal-improvements-pt-1), [connection-types](https://tailscale.com/kb/1257/connection-types), [device-connectivity](https://tailscale.com/docs/reference/device-connectivity), [portmapper pkg](https://pkg.go.dev/tailscale.com/net/portmapper); [NetBird NAT docs](https://docs.netbird.io/about-netbird/understanding-nat-and-connectivity), [PR #5219](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/pull/5219), issues [#3777](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/3777)/[#5789](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/5789)/[#2507](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird/issues/2507); [Headscale DERP](https://headscale.net/stable/ref/derp/), issue [#2846](https://github.com/juanfont/headscale/issues/2846); [Frikiteam benchmark](https://docs.frikiteam.es/en/doc/networking/tailscale_netbird_performance/).

corpus.summary: On macOS and iOS, Tailscale, Headscale-managed clients, and NetBird all use userspace WireGuard (wireguard-go) over Apple's utun kernel interface — because Apple has never shipped a kernel WireGuard module. When a connection is direct (no relay), the encrypted data-plane packet path is functionally identical: the same ChaCha20-Poly1305 cipher, the same wireguard-go engine, the same utun hop. Sub-millisecond per-packet latency differences between the three products on a direct path do not exist at the cipher or engine level. The only real data-plane differences that affect latency are (1) the absence of Linux GSO/GRO batch offloads on macOS (BatchSize=1 per system call, a throughput issue more than a latency issue), (2) Tailscale's optional gVisor netstack userspace-networking mode, which is NOT the default for regular macOS/iOS clients but does add measurable latency overhead if used, and (3) an unresolved NetBird iOS data-plane bug (April 2026, issue #5789) where data packets are not injected into or read from the utun interface, causing one-way connectivity. The performance comparison literature confirms sub-5% differences between NetBird and Tailscale in controlled direct-path benchmarks conducted on Linux; no macOS-specific benchmarks have been published.

points.claim: Apple ships no kernel WireGuard module — all WireGuard mesh tools on macOS/iOS are forced into userspace wireguard-go
points.detail: macOS and iOS have no in-kernel WireGuard implementation. Apple's platform provides only the utun socket interface (com.apple.net.utun_control) for VPN-like TUN devices, and WireGuard's own cross-platform guidance confirms the macOS implementation uses wireguard-go over utun. The WireGuard project explicitly notes that 'the utun driver does not yet support sticky sockets and won't support fwmarks because of Darwin limitations.' This applies equally to Tailscale, NetBird, and Headscale-managed clients — none of them have a kernel fast-path available on Apple platforms.

points.claim: wireguard-go tun_darwin.go implements BatchSize()=1 — no batch offload on macOS, unlike Linux which gets BatchSize=128 with GSO/GRO
points.detail: Source code in Tailscale's wireguard-go fork (the authoritative implementation used by all three products on macOS) shows: tun_darwin.go returns BatchSize()=1, meaning exactly one packet per system-call read/write cycle. The Linux tun_linux.go returns a dynamic batchSize that resolves to conn.IdealBatchSize=128 when IFF_VNET_HDR (virtio offload) is available (Linux ≥6.2). The StdNetBind.BatchSize() in conn/bind_std.go also hardcodes 'if runtime.GOOS == linux { return IdealBatchSize } return 1'. This is a throughput cap (more syscall overhead per byte), not a per-packet latency addition. For terminal text (SSH), this is irrelevant. For high-throughput video streaming it limits maximum bandwidth but still delivers sub-millisecond per-frame latency.

points.claim: The DefaultMTU for wireguard-go is 1420 on all platforms including macOS — identical across tools
points.detail: wireguard-go's device/tun.go defines const DefaultMTU = 1420 with no platform conditional. This is the standard WireGuard MTU for an IPv4 underlay with 1500-byte Ethernet MTU (1500 - 20 IP header - 8 UDP header - 32 WireGuard header = 1440, rounded conservatively to 1420). Both Tailscale and NetBird use this same default. MTU mismatches — not defaults — are the source of fragmentation-induced latency, and all three tools use the same value.

points.claim: Tailscale's 'userspace-networking' mode (gVisor netstack) is a container/serverless feature — NOT the default for macOS or iOS Tailscale clients
points.detail: Tailscale documents that userspace-networking mode (--tun=userspace-networking) replaces the TUN device with gVisor's netstack TCP/IP stack. This mode is explicitly designed for environments like Heroku and Google Cloud Run that cannot access /dev/net/tun, and is 'primarily for serverless environments.' Regular macOS clients — App Store variant (Network Extension), Standalone variant (System Extension), and tailscaled (utun) — all use a real TUN interface backed by wireguard-go, not gVisor netstack. The gVisor path adds measurable latency: a documented issue (#18916) shows Nagle+delayed-ACK in gVisor's gonet.TCPConn causing ~220ms delays. This penalty only applies if a user explicitly invokes --tun=userspace-networking, which is not the default on any Apple platform.

points.claim: Headscale-managed Tailscale clients use identical data-plane code — the control plane replacement changes nothing in the data path
points.detail: Headscale is a drop-in replacement for Tailscale's coordination server only. Clients connecting to Headscale are unmodified official Tailscale clients (macOS App, tailscaled, iOS app). The data plane — wireguard-go, magicsock, MagicDNS — is the same binary. WireGuard tunnels are established directly between peers, never transiting the control server. Therefore Headscale vs. Tailscale-cloud is a control-plane and relay-server question, not a data-plane question.

points.claim: NetBird uses wireguard-go on macOS (kernel WireGuard on Linux when available) — identical data-plane crypto path to Tailscale on Apple platforms
points.detail: NetBird's architecture documentation confirms it manages WireGuard interfaces; on Linux it defaults to kernel mode but supports userspace. On macOS and iOS, no kernel WireGuard is available, so NetBird necessarily falls back to wireguard-go over utun — the same userspace path as Tailscale. NetBird supports a NB_USE_NETSTACK_MODE for Docker rootless, which uses gVisor netstack like Tailscale's userspace-networking mode, but this is also not the default for macOS/iOS clients.

points.claim: DERP relay is NOT in the data path once a direct connection is established — it handles only negotiation and fallback
points.detail: Tailscale's magicsock documentation confirms DERP serves two separate roles: (1) connection negotiation/upgrade bootstrap and (2) last-resort relay when NAT traversal fails. The MagicSock adaptive transport documentation states direct UDP packets travel PathDirectIPv4 or PathDirectIPv6 without MagicSock or DERP framing. Once bestAddr is established, DERP is not consulted for data packets. However, magicsock does maintain DERP connections for keepalives and path re-evaluation (heartbeatInterval=2s), which generates control traffic on the DERP connection but does not add latency to the direct data path.

points.claim: Controlled benchmarks confirm <5% latency/throughput differences between NetBird and Tailscale on direct P2P — but tests were Linux-only
points.detail: The most detailed published benchmark (Frikiteam docs, Ubuntu 22.04 AWS EC2) shows intra-region latency of 1.2ms±0.1ms (Tailscale) vs 1.1ms±0.1ms (NetBird) — a 0.1ms difference that is within measurement noise. Throughput was 897 Mbps vs 912 Mbps TCP single-stream. NetBird's own published benchmark (vs Cloudflare Mesh) similarly showed 'within normal network fluctuation.' Critically, these benchmarks were on Linux where NetBird may use kernel WireGuard while Tailscale uses wireguard-go — the kernel vs userspace gap is erased on macOS because both must use userspace. No macOS-specific benchmark data has been published by any of the three projects.

points.claim: Active NetBird iOS data-plane bug (April 2026) renders the iOS client non-functional for outbound traffic in affected configurations
points.detail: GitHub issue #5789 (filed April 2026, status unresolved in search results) reports that on iOS, WireGuard handshakes complete and ICE/relay signaling functions correctly, but zero data packets are injected into or read from the utun interface by the NetBird app. The result is one-way connectivity: other peers can reach the iOS device but the iPhone cannot send data to any peer. This occurs on both WiFi and cellular, with both P2P and forced relay. This is a real production risk for anyone relying on NetBird for iOS clients, and has no equivalent open issue in Tailscale's tracker.

points.claim: Linux wireguard-go with GSO/GRO now matches or exceeds kernel WireGuard throughput — but this optimization is Linux-only and irrelevant to macOS
points.detail: Tailscale's throughput improvements blog documents wireguard-go on Linux with UDP GSO/GRO (Linux ≥6.2) achieving 13.0 Gbps vs kernel WireGuard's 11.8 Gbps on bare metal i5-12400. The same optimizations are Linux-kernel-specific (TUNSETOFFLOAD, IFF_VNET_HDR, virtio_net_hdr) and are explicitly not implemented in tun_darwin.go. This means on Linux, Tailscale's wireguard-go can outperform in-kernel WireGuard for throughput; on macOS this debate is moot — both modes run identical userspace wireguard-go with BatchSize=1.

verdict.evidence:
## Data plane parity — confirmed, and the implication is decisive

All three mesh VPNs (Tailscale, Headscale, NetBird) use WireGuard userspace (wireguard-go). macOS and iOS have no kernel WireGuard. On a direct P2P path the data plane is identical: raw throughput benchmarks show differences of ≤2.5% that fall inside run-to-run noise (frikiteam benchmark, April 2026). "Overall faster" therefore reduces entirely to "more reliably direct".

---

## Adversarial verification: Tailscale iOS uses wireguard-go (not Apple's WireGuardKit)

**Verdict: CONFIRMED — wireguard-go, not Apple's WireGuardKit.**

Evidence from source code:

1. `/wgengine/userspace.go` imports `"github.com/tailscale/wireguard-go/device"` — the wireguard-go device is the WireGuard engine used on all platforms including darwin/iOS ([tailscale/tailscale@main, wgengine/userspace.go](https://github.com/tailscale/tailscale/blob/main/wgengine/userspace.go)).

2. `/wgengine/mem_ios.go` — an iOS-specific file that directly tunesqueue sizes on `device.*` from `"github.com/tailscale/wireguard-go/device"`: `device.QueueStagedSize = 64`, `device.PreallocatedBuffersPerPool = 64`, etc. This file exists specifically to reduce memory footprint for the iOS Network Extension ([tailscale/tailscale@main, wgengine/mem_ios.go](https://github.com/tailscale/tailscale/blob/main/wgengine/mem_ios.go)).

3. A search result note confirms: "wireguard-go memory pool deadlock issue was resolved" in recent Tailscale iOS versions — consistent with wireguard-go being the live runtime.

4. The Tailscale wiki explicitly says the macOS/iOS App Store version uses "Apple Network Extension API" for the *TUN interface* (utun), but the *WireGuard crypto engine inside that extension* is Tailscale's fork of wireguard-go, not Apple's WireGuardKit (WireGuardKit is a separate Swift/ObjC wrapper that calls into wireguard-go-bridge; Tailscale bypasses the Swift wrapper and calls wireguard-go directly in Go).

**Correction to the claim being verified:** The wiki's "Network Extension API" refers to how the utun device is obtained from the OS (via NEPacketTunnelProvider), not to Apple's WireGuardKit crypto library. The underlying WireGuard engine is wireguard-go, not Apple's Swift framework.

---

## The crux: NAT traversal mechanism comparison

### Tailscale NAT traversal stack (authoritative: [tailscale.com/blog/how-nat-traversal-works](https://tailscale.com/blog/how-nat-traversal-works))

Full pipeline, in order:

1. **STUN** — discovers public endpoint (IP:port) from each side.
2. **UDP hole punching** — simultaneous packet exchange; works for endpoint-independent ("easy") NATs.
3. **Port mapping protocols, attempted in parallel:**
   - **UPnP IGD** — consumer router protocol; requests explicit WAN→LAN port forward.
   - **NAT-PMP** (Apple's protocol, RFC 6886) — simpler, lower overhead.
   - **PCP** (Port Control Protocol, RFC 6887, NAT-PMP v2).
   Source: portmapper package, `/net/portmapper/portmapper.go` — package comment: "UDP port mapping client. It currently allows for mapping over NAT-PMP, UPnP, and PCP." Build tag: `!js` for UPnP; no restriction for PCP; applies to iOS and macOS builds where `buildfeatures.HasPortMapper` is true.
4. **Birthday paradox attack** — when facing symmetric (endpoint-dependent) NATs, probes many random destination ports simultaneously to statistically force a hit.
5. **ICE-style parallel probing** — tries everything simultaneously.
6. **DERP relay fallback** — encrypted relay over TLS/443, TCP-based. Always starts as initial carrier; connection upgrades to direct if NAT traversal succeeds. DERP relay latency: 50–282ms depending on server location.
7. **Peer relay (Oct 2025)** — dedicate a tailnet node as relay; lower latency than DERP, controllable capacity.

**"Hard NAT" failure condition:** If *both* peers are behind hard NAT (symmetric/endpoint-dependent, no port mapping support), Tailscale cannot establish direct. One hard-NAT peer + one easy-NAT peer can usually go direct via hole punching or port mapping.

**macOS/iOS-specific:** No platform-specific NAT traversal limitations documented. The portmapper (`UPnP/NAT-PMP/PCP`) runs on macOS and iOS where the feature flag `buildfeatures.HasPortMapper` is enabled; the only platform where it is stub-disabled in source is `js` (WebAssembly). The `mem_ios.go` tuning file confirms iOS gets the full wireguard-go engine, and the portmapper code path in `magicsock.go` checks only `runtime.GOOS != "js"` before using portmapper, meaning iOS and macOS are not excluded.

### NetBird NAT traversal stack (authoritative: [docs.netbird.io/about-netbird/understanding-nat-and-connectivity](https://docs.netbird.io/about-netbird/understanding-nat-and-connectivity), [netbirdio/netbird source](https://github.com/netbirdio/netbird))

1. **ICE (pion/ice v4)** — standard WebRTC ICE implementation. Three candidate types:
   - **Host candidates** — local IP:port.
   - **Server-reflexive (STUN/srflx)** — public IP:port via STUN binding request.
   - **Relay (TURN)** — NetBird relay server address.
   Candidate priority: host↔host > host↔srflx > srflx↔srflx > relay↔any.
2. **UDP hole punching** — via ICE connectivity checks.
3. **Relay fallback** — NetBird relay uses **QUIC (UDP) primary** and **WebSocket/TCP fallback**; races both simultaneously ([deepwiki.com/netbirdio/netbird/4-signal-and-relay-services](https://deepwiki.com/netbirdio/netbird/4-signal-and-relay-services)).

**Critical omission confirmed by source code and community reports:** NetBird **does NOT implement UPnP, NAT-PMP, or PCP port mapping**. The `ice/config.go` has no port-mapping field. The `ice/agent.go` uses `pion/ice` with only host, srflx, and relay candidate types — no `prflx` (peer-reflexive) via port mapping. A GitHub issue from May 2025 (netbird/netbird#3777) confirms explicitly: "while tailscale uses UPnP, PCP and NAT-PMP to try to open ports, NetBird doesn't." The docs mention these protocols only as manual workarounds the *user* can configure on their router, not as something the NetBird client actively uses.

---

## The practical consequence for Apple-native P2P reliability

| Scenario | Tailscale | NetBird |
|---|---|---|
| Both peers on easy NAT (full-cone / port-predictable) | Direct via hole punching | Direct via ICE hole punching |
| One peer on easy NAT, one behind CGNAT/ISP NAT | Direct: Tailscale gets explicit port mapping via NAT-PMP/UPnP on the easy side | Often falls back to relay: no port mapping to stabilize the mapping |
| Both peers behind hard NAT | Relay (DERP or peer relay) | Relay (TURN/NetBird relay) |
| Both peers on Apple home router (supports NAT-PMP natively — Apple AirPort, many third-party routers) | High direct probability: Tailscale probes NAT-PMP which Apple hardware supports well | Lower direct probability: no NAT-PMP probe |
| Post-sleep reconnect | DERP relay as initial path, re-upgrades to direct | ICE re-negotiation; same relay race |

**For the Apple home/office scenario specifically:** macOS and iOS devices are frequently behind consumer routers that support NAT-PMP (including Apple's own hardware historically, and most prosumer routers). Tailscale actively probes for NAT-PMP on the default gateway and requests explicit port mappings. NetBird does not. This means Tailscale has a meaningfully higher probability of establishing and *keeping* a stable direct connection in the home/office environment, not just in the initial handshake but also after reconnections.

---

## Role of UPnP / NAT-PMP / PCP

These protocols let the client ask the local router to install an explicit WAN→LAN port forwarding rule, which:
1. Converts an endpoint-dependent (hard) NAT into an endpoint-independent (easy) NAT for the mapped port.
2. Gives both peers a *stable, predictable* external port number that persists even when the far peer changes — so hole punching succeeds even from a fresh connection.
3. Provides a longer-lived mapping that survives idle periods (NAT-PMP/PCP have explicit lifetimes the client can renew).

Without port mapping, hole punching only works reliably when both NATs are already endpoint-independent. If one side is behind a carrier-grade or symmetric NAT, no port mapping means relay.

**Tailscale** probes UPnP IGD, NAT-PMP, and PCP in parallel on startup and refreshes mappings. **NetBird** relies on ICE without port mapping — pure hole punching.

---

## Headscale vs Tailscale (control plane only, same data plane)

Headscale is a self-hosted replacement for Tailscale's *control plane* only. The Tailscale client (including its magicsock, portmapper, DERP code) runs unchanged. Headscale ships an embedded DERP server and by default also includes Tailscale's public DERP servers ([headscale.net/stable/ref/derp/](https://headscale.net/stable/ref/derp/)). The embedded DERP has no "speed or throughput optimisations." NAT traversal behavior (portmapper, hole punching, birthday attack) is identical to Tailscale because the *client binary is identical* — Headscale only replaces the coordination server. The practical difference: Headscale's embedded DERP may have higher relay latency if the self-hosted server is farther from clients than Tailscale's geographically distributed DERP fleet.

---

## Summary verdict

- **On a confirmed direct P2P connection: performance is identical.** wireguard-go userspace is the engine in all cases on Apple platforms; no kernel WireGuard exists on macOS/iOS. Raw latency and throughput differences are within noise.
- **The real differentiator is direct connection reliability.** Tailscale has a substantially richer NAT traversal stack (STUN + hole punching + UPnP + NAT-PMP + PCP + birthday attack). NetBird has ICE with STUN/TURN but no port mapping protocols. For an Apple home/office environment where routers often support NAT-PMP, Tailscale is more likely to establish and maintain direct connections.
- **Tailscale's DERP relay is TCP-based (50–282ms in docs examples); NetBird's relay uses QUIC (UDP primary).** If you end up relayed, NetBird's relay transport is architecturally lower latency than Tailscale's DERP. But the goal is to avoid relay entirely.
- **For remote coding (terminal + video):** Getting and staying direct is what matters. Tailscale's port-mapping advantage is most relevant in mixed-NAT topologies (e.g., one end on a home router behind ISP NAT, one end on a cloud server). If both endpoints have stable public IPs or are on the same LAN, the difference is minimal.
- **Confidence caveat (medium, not high):** Direct connection success *rate* statistics are not publicly published by either project. The conclusion about Tailscale's advantage is mechanistic (it actively does port mapping, NetBird does not) and corroborated by the May 2025 field report in netbird#3777, but no controlled study of direct-connection-success-rate has been found.
verdict.correction: The claim is confirmed with one important correction to a common misreading: "Network Extension API" in the Tailscale wiki refers to how macOS/iOS obtains the utun TUN device (via Apple's NEPacketTunnelProvider), NOT to Apple's WireGuardKit Swift library. Tailscale's WireGuard crypto engine on iOS and macOS is wireguard-go (Tailscale's fork), confirmed by direct source code — mem_ios.go tunes wireguard-go device queue sizes specifically for the iOS Network Extension memory limit. Apple's WireGuardKit/wireguard-apple is a separate project that Tailscale does not use.

corpus.summary: Tailscale and NetBird both use WireGuard as the data plane, so on a live direct connection latency is identical. The real differentiator is which tool more reliably achieves and keeps a direct connection rather than falling to relay. Tailscale has historically been more aggressive — it deploys UPnP-IGD, NAT-PMP, and PCP port-mapping alongside STUN endpoint discovery, ICE-style simultaneous hole-punching, and a birthday-paradox spray for symmetric NAT. NetBird relied exclusively on ICE/STUN hole-punching with no port-mapping protocols until April 2026, when PCP (falling back to NAT-PMP/UPnP) was merged and shipped in v0.69.0. Both fall back to a relay when direct fails, but the relays differ in design. Headscale uses the exact same Tailscale client-side NAT traversal code; its only difference is a self-hosted control/DERP plane.

points.claim: Both macOS and iOS run wireguard-go (userspace), not kernel WireGuard — this is equal across Tailscale and NetBird
points.detail: Apple provides no in-kernel WireGuard module. The macOS/iOS Tailscale app wraps wireguard-go via the Network Extension API (App Store build) or /dev/utun (tailscaled CLI build). NetBird does the same on Apple platforms. Because the data plane is identical, raw per-packet latency on a live direct P2P path is the same for both tools. The performance gap between userspace and kernel WireGuard is typically under 5% throughput and immeasurable on latency at interactive coding traffic volumes.

points.claim: Tailscale's NAT traversal stack: STUN + UDP hole-punching + birthday-paradox spray + UPnP-IGD + NAT-PMP + PCP + DERP relay-then-upgrade
points.detail: The full Tailscale mechanism sequence is: (1) STUN endpoint discovery — each peer queries DERP nodes (which double as STUN servers) to learn its public ip:port. (2) Simultaneous UDP hole-punching / simultaneous open — both peers send packets to each other's discovered endpoints at the same time so each NAT sees an outbound flow and admits the return. (3) Birthday-paradox / spray technique for endpoint-dependent (symmetric) NAT — one side opens ~256 ephemeral ports while the other probes random destinations, reducing the search space from 65,535 to a manageable probability; the blog reports 99.9% success within ~20 s at 100 pps. (4) Port-mapping protocols — Tailscale's magicsock actively attempts UPnP-IGD (SOAP/XML, UDP 1900 discovery), NAT-PMP (Apple-originated, RFC 6886, UDP 5351), and PCP (RFC 6887, NAT-PMP v2) in parallel against the local gateway. A successful mapping gives the peer a stable, publicly reachable ip:port that bypasses hole-punching entirely. The portmapper monitors epoch fields to auto-renew after router reboots. These are compiled into every non-JS platform including darwin (macOS) and iOS (no ts_omit_portmapper build tag). (5) DERP relay — HTTP/TLS-based relay that starts immediately on connection establishment and transparently backs off once a direct path is found (relay-then-upgrade). DERP also serves as the side-channel for coordinating simultaneous probing.

points.claim: NetBird's NAT traversal stack: ICE (RFC 8445) via pion/ice with host + srflx + relay candidates, STUN for srflx discovery, custom QUIC/WebSocket relay — no UPnP/NAT-PMP/PCP until v0.69.0 (April 2026)
points.detail: NetBird uses the pion/ice v4 library (WebRTC ICE). It gathers three candidate types: host (local interface IPs), server reflexive / srflx (public ip:port from STUN query to stun.netbird.io), and relay (TURN/NetBird relay addresses). ICE candidate exchange happens over the Signal service (TCP 443). UDP hole-punching is attempted simultaneously. If that fails, NetBird falls to its custom relay (turn.netbird.io), which supports QUIC as primary and WebSocket over TCP/443 as fallback — unlike Tailscale's HTTP/TLS DERP, NetBird's relay is QUIC-first. Critically, NetBird had zero UPnP-IGD, NAT-PMP, or PCP code in its codebase through early 2026 (confirmed by full tree search returning no portmapper files). PR #5219 ('Add PCP support'), merged 2026-04-15, added PCP-first with NAT-PMP/UPnP fallback, shipped in v0.69.0 on 2026-04-20. For the user's current NetBird deployment (pre-v0.69.0), this portmapping layer is absent.

points.claim: The UPnP/NAT-PMP/PCP delta was the historical mechanism gap — it is now closed in NetBird v0.69.0+
points.detail: Up to NetBird v0.68.x (April 2026), the claim 'Tailscale uses UPnP-IGD + NAT-PMP + PCP while NetBird does NOT' was accurate and confirmed in NetBird GitHub issues (#3777, #4047). NetBird v0.69.0 (2026-04-20) closes this gap: PCP is now attempted first, falls back to NAT-PMP, then UPnP-IGD, mirroring Tailscale's portmapper behavior. The implementation also includes epoch monitoring for auto-renewal after router reboots — the same feature Tailscale added in its own NAT traversal improvements pt-1 blog post. However, NetBird still does not implement the birthday-paradox spray technique for symmetric NAT (no evidence of this in the codebase), which Tailscale applies as an additional layer.

points.claim: Tailscale achieves direct connections in more than 90% of cases; relay fallback adds 20–50 ms latency (the 'relay latency cliff')
points.detail: Tailscale's own telemetry and blog post report 'success rates for direct NAT traversal well north of 90% in typical conditions' — meaning fewer than ~10% of connections fall back to DERP relay. DERP relay adds 20–50 ms in typical geographies but can add 80–280 ms when the nearest DERP is distant. The October 2025 'Peer Relay' feature (designate a tailnet node as relay) offers an intermediate tier: lower latency than DERP (3–5 ms reported in same-region cases vs 50–280 ms for DERP) but still above zero-hop direct. The connection sequence is always: start on DERP → attempt upgrade to direct → if blocked, optionally use Peer Relay → stay on DERP as last resort.

points.claim: Same-LAN (local network) behavior: Tailscale directly detects local endpoints; NetBird has had documented cases of routing same-LAN traffic via relay or public internet
points.detail: Tailscale gathers LAN (host) candidates as part of its disco probing — if both peers are on the same subnet, the host candidate wins immediately and no NAT traversal is needed. NetBird uses ICE's host candidate priority (highest in ICE spec), which should also select a direct LAN path. However, GitHub issues #1753, #3777, and #3669 document real-world cases where NetBird peers on the same LAN connected via their public (srflx) endpoints or relay instead of host-to-host. The root cause in several reports is NAT hairpinning failure: when both peers share a NAT gateway, the gateway must support hairpinning (loopback of traffic to an internal IP via the public IP) for the srflx candidate to work from inside; if it does not, and if the host candidate discovery is also misconfigured (e.g., interface blacklist, wrong interface), the ICE agent falls to relay. Tailscale's portmapper and disco mechanism are more robust at recovering from this because a UPnP/NAT-PMP mapping gives a stable external port that works regardless of hairpin support.

points.claim: Symmetric / hard NAT: Tailscale has the birthday-paradox spray as an additional weapon; NetBird falls to relay
points.detail: Symmetric NAT (endpoint-dependent mapping) assigns a different external port for each destination, breaking standard STUN-based hole-punching. Tailscale's blog documents the birthday-paradox technique: one peer opens ~256 source ports simultaneously while the other probes random destinations, reducing the combined search space via birthday-paradox probability math to achieve ~99.9% success within ~20 s. Additionally, Tailscale sponsored a FreeBSD PF patch for endpoint-independent NAT mapping that benefits router operators. NetBird's pion/ice library does not implement a birthday-paradox spray; for symmetric NAT it falls straight to the QUIC/WebSocket relay. Both tools ultimately relay under strict symmetric NAT, but Tailscale has a probabilistic path to avoiding the relay cliff.

points.claim: Headscale is NAT-traversal-identical to Tailscale — it uses the same official Tailscale clients with all the same magicsock/portmapper code
points.detail: Headscale is a self-hosted reimplementation of the Tailscale coordination server protocol. All official Tailscale clients (including macOS and iOS) connect to Headscale without modification. The NAT traversal code — magicsock, portmapper (UPnP/NAT-PMP/PCP), disco probing, birthday-paradox spray, DERP relay-then-upgrade — is 100% shared with the Tailscale SaaS path. The only difference is the control plane origin and DERP server geography. Headscale ships an embedded DERP server but it lacks captive portal support and has no geographic distribution, meaning relay fallback latency depends entirely on where the operator places the Headscale server.

points.claim: Role of UPnP-IGD / NAT-PMP / PCP in direct-connection probability: port-mapping bypasses hole-punching entirely by giving the peer a stable external address
points.detail: Port-mapping protocols (UPnP-IGD, NAT-PMP, PCP) work by asking the local gateway router to install an explicit forwarding rule: 'forward external-port X to internal-ip:port Y'. If the router supports and honors the request, the peer's WireGuard port becomes reachable from the internet without any hole-punching needed. This eliminates the failure modes of symmetric NAT, short NAT timeouts, and hairpin failures. Tailscale has implemented these since early in its history (pkg comment: 'currently allows for mapping over NAT-PMP, UPnP, and PCP'). For home/SOHO routers — the environment for a remote-coding tool where the macOS host is at home — UPnP and/or NAT-PMP are typically enabled by default, making this a reliable direct-connection path. Corporate/cloud firewalls virtually never support these protocols, so hole-punching is the only option there. The combination of portmapping + STUN + spray is why Tailscale's 90%+ direct rate is achievable.

verdict.claim: Tailscale's portmapper (UPnP/NAT-PMP/PCP) is enabled in the distributed macOS App Store and iOS App Store builds — the build tag ts_omit_portmapper does not appear in Apple distribution builds.
verdict.evidence:
Two independent code paths confirm this.

1. The Tailscale build system (`tool/gocross/autoflags.go`, lines 123–180) is the sole place where build tags are auto-injected for iOS/macOS Xcode builds. For `GOOS=ios` and `GOOS=darwin` with `XCODE_VERSION_ACTUAL` set (i.e., App Store builds), only three tags are added: `ts_macext`, `omitidna`, and `omitpemdecrypt`. `ts_omit_portmapper` is NOT in this list.

2. The iOS dependency test (`tstest/iosdeps/iosdeps_test.go`) explicitly lists packages that must NOT be present in iOS builds. `tailscale.com/net/portmapper` is not in the `BadDeps` list, meaning its presence is accepted/expected. The test runs with `GOOS=ios, GOARCH=arm64` and imports `tailscale.com/feature/condregister` — the condregister for portmapper (`feature/condregister/portmapper/maybe_portmapper.go`) has the build constraint `//go:build !ts_omit_portmapper`, which is satisfied (portmapper is active) unless the omit tag is explicitly set, and it isn't for Apple builds.

3. The WASM comment in `cmd/tsconnect/wasmbuild/wasmbuild.go` explicitly explains why portmapper IS omitted for js/wasm (`js/wasm has no UDP sockets, can't speak NAT-PMP / PCP / UPnP`) — this same reasoning does NOT apply to iOS or macOS, confirming the omission is platform-capability-based, not policy-based for Apple platforms.

CAVEAT (load-bearing): Whether the portmapper successfully opens port mappings at runtime on an iOS device is a separate question from whether it is compiled in. Apple's Network Extension sandbox restricts certain raw socket operations. The Tailscale macOS docs note that sandboxed App Store builds have limitations (e.g., no Tailscale SSH server). Whether the portmapper's UDP multicast/unicast probing for UPnP/NAT-PMP/PCP is actually reachable from within the iOS Network Extension sandbox has not been confirmed by network capture evidence in the sources found. This remains claims_to_verify via live device packet capture.
verdict.correction: No correction needed on the core claim. The build tag `ts_omit_portmapper` is confirmed absent from Apple distribution builds. The partial caveat in the original claim ("this should be verified by inspecting the actual Xcode/iOS build scripts or a network capture") is now answered for the build-script dimension: the Xcode build scripts confirm portmapper is compiled in. The network-capture dimension (runtime effectiveness inside the iOS Network Extension sandbox) remains unconfirmed and should still be verified empirically.

verifications.claim: NetBird v0.69.0 PCP/NAT-PMP/UPnP support (PR #5219, merged 2026-04-15, shipped 2026-04-20) is actually present in the macOS and iOS client builds — the PR is Go code but Apple builds may have platform-specific exclusions

verdict.claim: NetBird v0.69.0 PCP/NAT-PMP/UPnP support (PR #5219, merged 2026-04-15, shipped 2026-04-20) is actually present in the macOS and iOS client builds — the PR is Go code but Apple builds may have platform-specific exclusions.
verdict.evidence: The portforward package build constraint is //go:build !js (js/wasm stub only). iOS uses GOOS=ios, which is NOT excluded by !js. The stub manager_js.go explicitly says "NAT-PMP/UPnP is not available in browser environments" — a js-only stub. The same !js pattern is used by Tailscale's portmapper. engine.go calls portforward.NewManager() unconditionally on all non-js platforms. The iOS path (RunOniOS → run → NewEngine) calls the same NewEngine that sets portForwardManager: portforward.NewManager(). No ios-specific engine variant or portforward exclusion exists in the codebase at HEAD. HOWEVER, two caveats reduce confidence from "high": (1) iOS kernel/sandbox restrictions on raw UDP sockets may prevent PCP/NAT-PMP from working at the OS level even if the Go code compiles and runs — this was not verified against actual iOS Network Extension sandbox entitlements; (2) the v0.69.0 release notes confirm PCP support was shipped, but the macOS routing and ICE path also received fixes in the same release, and a separate open issue (#5670, opened 2026-03-24) shows the macOS CLI daemon ICE agent never initializing on arm64/Homebrew, forcing all connections to relay — meaning even with PCP support present in the build, the macOS client may be relay-only due to a separate ICE initialization bug in daemon mode.
verdict.correction: The claim is directionally correct: the Go code is not platform-excluded from macOS or iOS builds. The build gate is !js only, not !ios or !darwin. PCP/NAT-PMP/UPnP support shipped in v0.69.0 (released 2026-04-20, PR #5219 merged 2026-04-15) and compiles into both macOS and iOS targets. The correction is: the iOS Network Extension sandbox may silently prevent actual UDP multicast/broadcast needed for UPnP/NAT-PMP gateway discovery at runtime — this is an unverified runtime risk, not a compile-time exclusion. Additionally, the macOS CLI daemon has an open ICE initialization bug (issue #5670) that forces relay-only connections regardless of port mapping support.

corpus.summary: Headscale is strictly a control-plane reimplementation; the data plane is the official Tailscale client (tailscaled / the App Store app), which inherits Tailscale's full NAT-traversal stack verbatim. DERP relay behavior, direct-connection probability, and per-platform WireGuard implementation are therefore identical to Tailscale-SaaS on the data path. The meaningful differences live in control-plane availability, DERP relay topology choices, a handful of SaaS features not yet ported, and one significant regression in v0.27.0 (now patched) that forced all connections through relay.

points.claim: Headscale is a control-plane-only reimplementation; the data plane is 100% the official Tailscale client
points.detail: Headscale replaces only the Tailscale coordination server (key exchange, ACL distribution, peer discovery, DERP region selection). All encrypted WireGuard traffic flows peer-to-peer or through DERP relays — the same code paths as Tailscale-SaaS. This means every NAT-traversal mechanism (STUN, UDP hole-punching, birthday-paradox multi-port probing, UPnP/NAT-PMP/PCP port mapping, DERP relay, IPv6 native path) is available identically under Headscale, because none of those live in the control server.

points.claim: On macOS and iOS, both Tailscale-SaaS and Headscale use userspace wireguard-go (not kernel WireGuard); raw data-plane performance is therefore identical
points.detail: Apple ships no kernel WireGuard module. The App Store Tailscale client uses Apple's Network Extension API with a utun interface. The CLI tailscaled uses /dev/utun directly. Both route through the wireguard-go userspace library. Because Headscale clients are the exact same Tailscale binaries, there is no WireGuard-layer performance difference between using Headscale vs Tailscale-SaaS on macOS/iOS.

points.claim: Headscale uses Tailscale's public DERP servers by default; self-hosting DERP is opt-in and optional
points.detail: By default, Headscale fetches and distributes Tailscale's public DERP map to all clients. If you enable the embedded DERP server (derp.server.enabled: true), it is added to the public DERP list rather than replacing it. To operate entirely on your own infrastructure, you must set derp.urls: [] to stop fetching Tailscale's public map. The embedded DERP is disabled by default. The docs explicitly warn: 'Removing Tailscale's DERP servers means there is now just a single DERP server available for clients. This is a single point of failure.'

points.claim: Self-hosted DERP latency depends entirely on geography, not on whether the relay binary is Headscale-embedded or Tailscale's derper
points.detail: Both Headscale's embedded DERP and Tailscale's standalone derper binary are relay-only: they forward encrypted WireGuard packets and cannot decrypt them. There are no speed or throughput optimizations in Headscale's embedded DERP — it is functionally equivalent to Tailscale's derper. Relay latency is therefore a function of where you deploy the relay relative to your clients, not which software runs the relay. A self-hosted DERP co-located with your users (e.g., same datacenter or ISP region) will have lower relay latency than Tailscale's geographically distributed public DERP only if your nearest public DERP happens to be farther than your VPS. For a two-node Apple-only setup where both endpoints are typically near each other, relay latency only matters during the brief relay phase before a direct upgrade, or when NAT traversal fails entirely.

points.claim: DERP relay latency cliff is real and documented: ~50–450ms on relay vs ~1–50ms direct, depending on geography
points.detail: Tailscale's own docs show tailscale ping examples with DERP-relayed latency of 50–282ms and direct connections at 35–50ms. Community reports show geographically unfavorable DERP hops (e.g., Delhi-to-Minneapolis) producing 452ms relay latency vs 298–306ms on a peer relay. Peer relays also showed a 12.5x throughput improvement over DERP relay. For a local-network or same-region two-machine setup, direct P2P at 1–5ms is the target; DERP relay fallback would be 50–200ms+ depending on the nearest DERP node. This is the 'latency cliff' that makes relay detection and avoidance the primary differentiator between mesh VPN solutions.

points.claim: Headscale v0.27.0 had a critical regression that forced ALL connections through DERP relay; patched in subsequent releases
points.detail: Issue #2846 (filed November 1 2025): Headscale v0.27.0 was not storing or propagating node endpoint information to peers. The Addrs field in the network map was null, so clients could not attempt direct UDP connections at all. All traffic was forced through DERP relay, producing 30ms+ latency even between same-subnet machines that should see <5ms. v0.27.1 addressed authentication/policy bugs; v0.28.0 introduced 'smarter partial map updates instead of full network maps' which is likely the upstream fix. Anyone running Headscale should be on v0.28.0 or later. Verify with tailscale status to confirm endpoint addresses are populated.

points.claim: macOS App Store Tailscale client supports Headscale via custom login server through a non-obvious UI path
points.detail: macOS GUI method: Option-click the Tailscale menu bar icon → Debug menu → 'Add Account...' under Custom Login Server → enter headscale URL. There is a documented caveat: this UI option only works 'when at least one other tailnet has been authenticated in the client.' If the device has never been connected to any tailnet, the CLI method must be used: tailscale login --login-server=https://your-headscale.example.com. The Tailscale Settings → Accounts → dropdown method also works but has the same prerequisite. The standalone tailscaled daemon has no such constraint and accepts --login-server at any time.

points.claim: iOS App Store Tailscale client supports Headscale natively since version 1.38.1 via 'Use custom coordination server'
points.detail: iOS setup: Launch Tailscale app → account icon (top right) → 'Log in...' → options menu → 'Use custom coordination server' → enter headscale URL. Alternatively, go to iOS Settings → Tailscale → 'ALTERNATE COORDINATION SERVER URL'. The minimum required version is 1.38.1, which was the first iOS release to support alternate control servers. Current App Store versions are well past this threshold. No other iOS-specific caveats are documented by Headscale.

points.claim: Control plane availability is a new single-point-of-failure with Headscale that Tailscale-SaaS eliminates
points.detail: Tailscale-SaaS runs a geo-distributed, highly available control plane. With Headscale on a single VPS, a VPS outage means: (1) no new connections can be established, (2) no key rotations or policy updates, though (3) existing, already-established WireGuard tunnels with cached policy continue working. For a remote-coding tool where you need to reconnect after a reboot or network change, control-plane availability matters more than for always-on tunnels. If you also disable Tailscale's public DERP in favor of a self-hosted relay on the same VPS, a single VPS failure takes down both control plane and relay simultaneously.

points.claim: Headscale supports all the NAT-traversal-adjacent features needed for low-latency direct connections: subnet routers, exit nodes, MagicDNS, Taildrop, Funnel, Serve, ephemeral nodes
points.detail: As of v0.28.0, the Headscale features page explicitly lists: subnet routers with auto-approval, exit nodes with ACL-based auto-approval, MagicDNS with split DNS and search domains, Taildrop file sharing, Tailscale SSH, Funnel, Serve, network flow logs, and ephemeral nodes. The notable gap is OIDC groups in ACLs (not supported), single OIDC provider per instance, and no polished admin web UI (CLI/API only). None of the missing features affect direct-connection reliability or NAT traversal.

points.claim: Headscale's route failover detection can lag up to 16 minutes to mark a node offline, vs near-instant for Tailscale-SaaS
points.detail: This is a documented behavioral difference: Tailscale-SaaS uses its always-on coordination plane to quickly detect node disappearance and propagate failover. Headscale's polling-based detection of offline nodes can take up to 16 minutes in some configurations. For a two-node remote-coding setup this is less critical (there is no HA subnet router failover needed), but it does mean that stale endpoint information may persist longer under Headscale, potentially contributing to longer relay-vs-direct resolution time after a network topology change.

points.claim: Summary trade-off: Headscale + self-hosted DERP vs Tailscale-SaaS for Apple-only low-latency remote coding
points.detail: You gain: full data sovereignty, no Tailscale metadata, no free-tier node limits, ability to restrict DERP relay to tailnet-only clients. You lose: HA control plane, Tailscale's geographically distributed public DERP fleet as free relay fallback (or you keep it by not setting urls: []), and polished admin UI. For the direct-connection path (which is the latency-critical path for terminal/GUI traffic), there is zero performance difference — both use identical Tailscale client code on macOS/iOS with wireguard-go. The only latency impact of Headscale vs Tailscale-SaaS is on the relay fallback path, and only if you've disabled public DERP and your self-hosted DERP is geographically worse than the nearest public DERP node. The primary operational risk is control plane downtime blocking new connections, and the historical endpoint-propagation regression in v0.27.0.

Tailscale reports >90% direct-connection success rate 'in typical conditions' — this is their own blog claim (nat-traversal-improvements-pt-1), not an independent benchmark; your specific NAT topology (especially if one end is behind CGNAT or a hard/symmetric NAT) may produce very different results
